key_insight: Treating PINN and FEM solvers as Steklov–Poincaré operators lets Robin–Neumann coupling stabilize interface exchange and yields a contraction guarantee even when the PINN is only approximately trained.
engineering_relevance: This provides a practical, meshless way to couple neural PDE solvers with FEM across moving interfaces and contact problems, reducing remeshing needs and improving robustness in fluid–structure interaction.
method: View each subdomain solver as a trace-to-flux operator.
Replace unstable Dirichlet–Neumann coupling with Robin–Neumann coupling using a closed-form interface impedance.
Use a Fourier-mode interface probe to estimate PINN resolvable interface eigenvalues and diagnose spectral limits.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports agreement between theory and experiments: predicted PINN–FEM contraction rates match measured rates within 7% on 1D/2D Poisson tests, and contact reaction error is 0.4% under mesh refinement.
watch_item: The PINN can drift off the Stokes manifold over long time horizons, and under-resolved pre-impact squeeze-film effects indicate a risk of missing sharp interface dynamics.
paper_title: Robin-Neumann Coupling of PINN and FEM Solvers: A Steklov-Poincaré View, with Application to Fluid-Structure Interaction with Contact
